title: Cuban Missile Crisis
summary: On the morning of 16 October 1962 President John F. Kennedy was shown aerial photographs taken over Cuba by a U-2 spyplane. They revealed Soviet medium-range ballistic missile sites under construction some 90 miles from Florida. Over the following thirteen days the United States and the Soviet Union confronted one another across the Caribbean; American warships blockaded Cuba, Soviet ships turned back, and, on 27 October, a Soviet surface-to-air missile shot down an American U-2 over the island. On 28 October Nikita Khrushchev announced that the missiles would be withdrawn. The world had come closer to nuclear war than at any other point in the Cold War.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The crisis grew out of a Cuban revolution and a superpower arms race. Fidel Castro's guerrilla movement had entered Havana on 1 January 1959 and overthrown the American-backed dictator Fulgencio Batista. Castro's early nationalisations, his alignment with the Soviet Union, and his declaration in April 1961 that Cuba was a socialist state made the island the first communist government in the western hemisphere. In April 1961 the Eisenhower-planned, Kennedy-executed Bay of Pigs invasion by CIA-trained Cuban exiles failed disastrously; Castro's grip tightened, and Havana asked Moscow for help against the American invasion Cuba's leaders regarded as inevitable.

The Soviet Union under Nikita Khrushchev had been claiming rough nuclear parity with the United States but in fact possessed only a handful of intercontinental ballistic missiles able to reach American soil, and it was known that the Americans knew this. American Jupiter intermediate-range ballistic missiles installed in Italy and, from early 1962, in Turkey, put Moscow within roughly ten minutes of nuclear strike. In May 1962, in his Black Sea dacha at Pitsunda, Khrushchev conceived the plan of deploying Soviet medium-range and intermediate-range ballistic missiles secretly to Cuba, arguing that it would both deter an American invasion of the island and even the strategic balance. Castro, initially hesitant, agreed on strategic grounds.

Operation Anadyr, disguised as a shipment of agricultural supplies, dispatched over the summer and autumn of 1962 a Soviet expeditionary force of some 40,000 troops, 24 R-12 medium-range and 16 R-14 intermediate-range ballistic missile launchers, a regiment of tactical nuclear cruise missiles, IL-28 nuclear-capable bombers, MiG-21 fighters, and four Foxtrot-class submarines each armed with a nuclear-tipped torpedo. American U-2 flights had detected surface-to-air missile deployments through the summer but flights over the western end of Cuba, where the ballistic missiles were being installed, had been suspended in early September to avoid political embarrassment. It was not until 14 October that a U-2 piloted by Major Richard Heyser overflew San Cristobal in Pinar del Rio and captured images that CIA analysts, working through the night, identified as the launch pads and support equipment of Soviet R-12 medium-range ballistic missiles.

image.caption: U-2 reconnaissance photograph of a Soviet missile base under construction in Cuba, shown to Kennedy on 16 October 1962.
image.credit: US Central Intelligence Agency / JFK Presidential Library, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
President Kennedy was shown the photographs on the morning of 16 October 1962 in the White House residence. He convened a small group of advisers, later formalised as the Executive Committee of the National Security Council (ExComm), which met almost continuously for the next thirteen days. Options ranged from doing nothing, through diplomatic protest, to air strikes on the missile sites, a full invasion of Cuba, or a naval blockade. Air Force generals led by Curtis LeMay pressed for immediate air strikes; Attorney General Robert Kennedy argued that a surprise attack was morally unlike the United States, invoking a 'Pearl Harbor in reverse'. By the end of the week the president had settled on a naval quarantine, called that rather than a blockade to avoid the technical language of an act of war, combined with the demand that the missiles be withdrawn.

On the evening of 22 October Kennedy addressed the nation on television, revealing the presence of the missiles, announcing the quarantine to take effect two days later, and warning that any missile launched from Cuba against any nation in the western hemisphere would be treated as a Soviet attack on the United States, requiring a full retaliatory response. Around the world US forces went to DEFCON 3; the Strategic Air Command placed its long-range bombers on airborne alert; American troops massed in Florida for a possible invasion. The Organization of American States endorsed the quarantine unanimously. Soviet cargo ships bearing further military equipment turned back short of the quarantine line on 24 October in the first easing of the confrontation. But construction on the missile sites already in Cuba continued, and the president's advisers agreed that within a week or two the missiles would be operational.

The most dangerous day was Saturday 27 October, known as Black Saturday. That morning a Soviet surface-to-air missile shot down a U-2 over eastern Cuba, killing pilot Major Rudolf Anderson, the sole American combat fatality of the crisis. Another U-2 strayed into Soviet airspace over Chukotka; Soviet fighters scrambled to intercept it. A US Navy destroyer dropping practice depth charges on the Soviet Foxtrot submarine B-59, exhausted after days submerged in warm Caribbean waters, came close to triggering the release of the submarine's nuclear-tipped torpedo; only the refusal of Vasili Arkhipov, the flotilla's second-in-command aboard, blocked the launch. That evening Robert Kennedy met Soviet ambassador Anatoly Dobrynin at the Justice Department and privately conveyed the terms of settlement: the United States would publicly pledge not to invade Cuba, and would privately, but not publicly, withdraw its Jupiter missiles from Turkey. On the morning of Sunday 28 October Radio Moscow broadcast Khrushchev's letter accepting the terms; the missiles would be dismantled and withdrawn.

image.caption: President Kennedy meets with the ExComm during the Cuban Missile Crisis, October 1962.
image.credit: Cecil Stoughton / JFK Presidential Library, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Dismantling proceeded through November under UN supervision, although Fidel Castro, who had been kept largely out of the endgame, refused inspections on Cuban soil; verification was done from air and sea. In exchange the American Jupiter missiles were quietly withdrawn from Turkey and Italy the following spring. The Soviet IL-28 bombers were also removed after further negotiation. The formal quarantine ended on 20 November 1962; Kennedy's pledge not to invade Cuba, though never signed as a treaty, was substantially observed by his successors.

The crisis had immediate and durable strategic consequences. The Moscow-Washington 'hotline', a direct teletype link between the Kremlin and the Pentagon, was installed in 1963 to reduce the risk of a similar crisis. In August 1963 the two powers, together with Britain, signed the Partial Test Ban Treaty prohibiting nuclear tests in the atmosphere, in outer space, and under water. Broader arms-control negotiations followed through the 1960s and 1970s: the Non-Proliferation Treaty (1968), the ABM Treaty and SALT I (1972), and SALT II (1979). Khrushchev's political standing at home, already weakened by economic setbacks and by his handling of Berlin, was fatally damaged; he was removed by the Politburo in October 1964. Kennedy was reinforced politically at home, though his own life was cut short by an assassin in Dallas on 22 November 1963.

For Cuba the crisis produced a lasting alignment with Moscow, buffered by Soviet economic subsidies until the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991. The American embargo, tightened after 1962, remained in place for the rest of the century. The events in the Caribbean shaped the doctrines and cultural fears of the nuclear age: films from Dr Strangelove (1964) to Fail-Safe and The Day After returned to their scenarios; nuclear civil defence programmes, backyard fallout shelters, and school 'duck and cover' drills persisted for years. In Latin America, the crisis fed both Cuba-inspired revolutionary movements from Bolivia to Uruguay and American counter-insurgency doctrines and interventions that shaped the region into the 1980s.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Cuban Missile Crisis is widely taught in international relations as the paradigm case of crisis management. Kennedy's ExComm meetings, secretly recorded by a hidden Oval Office taping system, have provided historians and political scientists with an unusually rich record of high-level decision-making. Robert Kennedy's memoir Thirteen Days (1969) shaped the popular narrative. Graham Allison's Essence of Decision (1971), which offered three competing models (rational actor, organisational process, governmental politics) for reading the same events, became one of the most influential works in postwar political science.

The declassification of Soviet and Cuban archives after 1989, and successive Havana conferences that brought together surviving American, Soviet, and Cuban participants including Robert McNamara and Fidel Castro, complicated the older heroic narrative. It became clear that tactical nuclear weapons were already on the island under Soviet control, that Soviet field commanders in Cuba had authority to use them against an American invasion, that Cuban air defence commanders had shot down the U-2 without Moscow's authorisation, and that the submarine incident on 27 October was closer to disaster than the American side had ever known. What had looked like a triumph of cool calculation began to look partly like luck.

The crisis reshaped the nuclear age in two directions. It made both superpowers more cautious about direct confrontation, ushering in an era of limited detente, arms control, and proxy wars fought at lower levels of intensity. It also made non-proliferation a central goal of both. Every subsequent case in which a small state has flirted with acquiring nuclear weapons, North Korea, Iraq, Iran, has been argued in part through the lens of October 1962. That the world came so close to catastrophe, and stepped back, remains both a warning and, for many, a fragile hope.

worldContext.text: China in October 1962 was itself at war. On 20 October, four days after the crisis in Washington began, Chinese People's Liberation Army units launched a coordinated offensive across the disputed Himalayan border with India, driving Indian forces back in the Northeast Frontier Agency (later Arunachal Pradesh) and in Aksai Chin. The Sino-Indian War produced a humiliating Indian defeat and, by early November, a unilateral Chinese ceasefire and withdrawal. Domestically China was still recovering from the Great Leap Forward famine, and its rupture with the Soviet Union was already open.

India under Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru was reeling from the shock of the Chinese offensive. The Indian Army, ill-prepared and poorly equipped for high-altitude war, suffered heavy casualties. Nehru's confidence in his own foreign policy of non-alignment and Sino-Indian friendship ('Hindi-Chini bhai-bhai') collapsed. India would move over the following years to a closer relationship with the Soviet Union and to a serious rearmament programme. In Pakistan, General Ayub Khan's government watched carefully and moved to open its own relationship with Beijing.

The Middle East in October 1962 was in another of its recurring crises. In Yemen, a coup on 26 September had overthrown the imam and proclaimed a republic; Egyptian troops under Nasser and Saudi money were about to be drawn into a long civil war on opposite sides. Algeria had won independence from France in July 1962 after eight years of war; Ahmed Ben Bella was consolidating power in Algiers. In Iran, the Shah's White Revolution reform programme was being drafted for a January 1963 referendum.

Sub-Saharan Africa in October 1962 was in the middle of its wave of independence, with Uganda becoming the tenth British African colony to gain independence on 9 October under Milton Obote. Rwanda and Burundi had gained independence from Belgium in July. The Congo crisis continued, with UN forces engaged against Katanga's secession under Moise Tshombe. Kenya, Zanzibar, and Nyasaland (Malawi) were all moving towards independence in the next two years. South Africa's Nelson Mandela had been arrested in August and was awaiting trial.

Europe in October 1962 was gripped by the same nuclear anxiety as the rest of the world, and particularly by the memory of the 1961 Berlin crisis. In Rome the Second Vatican Council opened on 11 October 1962 under Pope John XXIII with 2,540 bishops in attendance, launching the reforms that would remake the Catholic Church. In West Germany the Spiegel Affair, a police raid on the offices of Der Spiegel news magazine on 26 October, triggered a political crisis that would eventually force Defence Minister Franz Josef Strauss from office. In France, President Charles de Gaulle survived an August assassination attempt at Petit-Clamart.

Australia and New Zealand supported the American position but were largely bystanders to the missile crisis. Prime Minister Robert Menzies expressed public solidarity with Kennedy. Both dominions were far more focused on developments closer to home: Indonesian confrontation over West New Guinea (which passed under UN administration in October 1962), the Malayan-Indonesian tensions that would erupt into Konfrontasi over the formation of Malaysia in 1963, and the growing American request for allied help in Vietnam. Australia's first military advisers to South Vietnam had arrived in August 1962.

figures.name: John F. Kennedy
figures.role: US president who chaired the ExComm and imposed the naval quarantine of Cuba
figures.credit: Cecil W. Stoughton, White House, Public Domain

figures.name: Nikita Khrushchev
figures.role: First Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, ordered the deployment of missiles to Cuba and then their withdrawal

figures.name: Fidel Castro
figures.role: Prime minister of Cuba, accepted the Soviet deployment and opposed the diplomatic settlement
figures.credit: Bernard Gotfryd, Library of Congress, Public Domain

figures.name: Robert F. Kennedy
figures.role: US Attorney General, close adviser to his brother and back-channel negotiator with Ambassador Dobrynin
figures.credit: Harry Warnecke, NY Daily News (1964), Public Domain

figures.name: Anatoly Dobrynin
figures.role: Soviet ambassador to the United States, principal diplomatic conduit during the crisis

figures.name: Vasili Arkhipov
figures.role: Soviet naval officer aboard submarine B-59 whose refusal to authorise a nuclear torpedo launch on 27 October is credited by many historians with preventing nuclear war

images.caption: One of the first U-2 reconnaissance images of Soviet missile bases under construction in Cuba, shown to President Kennedy on the morning of 16 October 1962.
images.credit: US Central Intelligence Agency / JFK Presidential Library, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: President Kennedy meets with the Executive Committee of the National Security Council (ExComm) during the Cuban Missile Crisis, October 1962.
images.credit: Cecil Stoughton, White House Photographs / John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: A US Navy P-2H Neptune patrol aircraft overflying a Soviet freighter carrying missile components away from Cuba, November 1962.
